agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant implementation details undefined. This is an early-stage product concept communicated informally at an all-hands meeting, not a formal specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given market/time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but unstated: the standard delivery price before dynamic adjustment is applied" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to base price based on current demand level" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted in real time based on current demand levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy defining how pricing works. A VP could decide to switch to batch pricing or scheduled pricing tiers. The system would not be corrupt without real-time adjustment — it would just be a different product.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand level changes, the price offered for new delivery requests reflects the updated demand within the system's defined real-time window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand is high, prices must increase relative to the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. A future strategy could invert this (e.g., loss-leader pricing during peaks to capture market share). It is a business decision, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'high', the calculated delivery price is strictly greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand is low, prices must decrease relative to the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same reasoning as BR-2 — this is a pricing strategy decision, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'low', the calculated delivery price is strictly less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for individual pricing decisions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a product design choice. Manual override capability may be needed for incidents or regulatory compliance. A VP could mandate human-in-the-loop approval above certain thresholds.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are applied programmatically without requiring human approval for each delivery quote"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Guardrails must exist to constrain the range of allowable price adjustments",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, an algorithmic error or demand spike could produce prices of $0 or $100,000 for a local delivery. This is a system integrity constraint — unbounded pricing can cause financial harm, regulatory violations, and reputational damage. The system is in a corrupt state if it can produce unconstrained prices.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery price can be calculated outside the defined minimum and maximum price bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The delivery price must always be greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically nonsensical for a courier service. It would mean paying customers to take deliveries. This is a fundamental domain constraint — a price <= 0 represents data corruption in a pricing system.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level and any set of inputs, the final calculated delivery price is strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing system must balance competitiveness with profitability — prices should not consistently fall below cost of delivery",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Profitability is a strategic business goal, not a system integrity constraint. Companies routinely operate below cost for market capture. A VP could explicitly authorize below-cost pricing in a specific market.",
      "testable_assertion": "Over a defined measurement period, average dynamic price per delivery meets or exceeds the average cost of delivery"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails cap the price at the defined maximum — the system must not produce exploitative pricing during emergencies, which may also have regulatory implications" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price decreases to the minimum guardrail floor but never reaches zero or negative" },
    { "scenario": "Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by CEO — system behavior when demand signal is lost is a critical gap. Fallback to base price is a reasonable default but is not specified." },
    { "scenario": "Simultaneous conflicting demand signals across overlapping zones", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — geographic granularity of demand measurement is not specified" },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flipping between high and low within seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — no mention of smoothing, dampening, or minimum hold periods for price changes. Without this, customers may see price flickering." },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price at quote time but demand changes before booking confirmation", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — price locking/honoring policy is not addressed. This is a major UX and trust concern." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during declared emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — no mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging statutes that would override dynamic pricing during emergencies." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing has direct revenue impact: correct implementation increases revenue during peak demand and maintains volume during troughs. Incorrect guardrails risk either leaving money on the table (too tight) or customer backlash and regulatory fines (too loose). The absence of defined thresholds makes financial modeling impossible at this stage.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand measurement infrastructure, pricing calculation engine, and monitoring. 'Fully automated' implies no pricing analysts in the loop, shifting operational burden to system reliability and observability. Failure modes of the pricing engine become business-critical incidents.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which requires clear communication to maintain trust. Price transparency, quote honoring policies, and perceived fairness are critical UX concerns entirely absent from this description.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Regulatory risk from price-gouging laws is unaddressed. Reputational risk from perceived unfairness (surge pricing backlash, as seen with ride-sharing). Financial risk from algorithm errors producing extreme prices. The vagueness of 'guardrails' and 'probably' signals this has not been thought through.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will pay variable prices — trust, transparency, and perceived fairness are major concerns. Price-sensitive customers may churn." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Dynamic pricing likely affects courier compensation or demand distribution. Not addressed at all in the description." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Need to define profitability thresholds, cost floors, and margin targets that feed into guardrail configuration." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review regulatory requirements per jurisdiction — price-gouging statutes, consumer protection laws, pricing disclosure requirements." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need policies for price disputes and refund eligibility." },
      { "group": "Operations/SRE", "impact": "Own reliability of a now business-critical real-time pricing engine. Failure modes need defined fallback behavior." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "This is an informal CEO statement at an all-hands, not a product specification. The description contains multiple uses of hedge language ('probably', 'some guardrails', 'don't go crazy') indicating the rules are not yet decided. Critical parameters are entirely undefined: guardrail thresholds, demand measurement methodology, geographic granularity, price-locking policy, fallback behavior, regulatory compliance strategy, and courier impact. The only things stated with certainty are the directional relationship between demand and price, and the desire for automation. Everything else requires product and legal decisions that have not been made.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices increase when demand is high (explicitly stated)",
      "Prices decrease when demand is low (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (explicitly stated, though details undefined)",
      "Price must be positive (fundamental domain constraint)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined",
      "Guardrail bounds — 'probably' and 'don't go crazy' are not specifications",
      "Geographic and temporal granularity of demand measurement",
      "Price-locking and quote-honoring policy",
      "Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements per jurisdiction",
      "Impact on courier compensation model",
      "Definition of 'real time' — seconds? minutes?",
      "Whether 'competitive' means matching specific competitors or market-rate benchmarking"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists against which adjustments are made (implied but never stated)",
      "Demand is measurable in a meaningful, real-time quantifiable way",
      "The pricing applies to new delivery requests, not already-booked deliveries",
      "Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor",
      "The system operates per delivery, not per customer or per route"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail thresholds are entirely undefined. 'Probably have some guardrails so prices don't go crazy' is not a specification. Without defined min/max bounds, the most critical safety mechanism of DynaPricing cannot be implemented or tested.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["A) Define absolute min/max price bounds (e.g., $2 floor, $50 ceiling)", "B) Define percentage-based bounds relative to base price (e.g., -30% to +200%)", "C) Define both absolute and percentage bounds, with the tighter constraint winning"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — use both absolute and percentage bounds. Absolute bounds prevent catastrophic edge cases; percentage bounds keep pricing proportional to delivery type. Requires Finance and Legal input to set actual values."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Demand measurement is undefined. 'Based on demand' requires a concrete definition: demand in what geographic area, over what time window, measured how (order volume vs. courier availability ratio vs. historical comparison)?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/SRE", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["A) Order volume in a zone over a rolling time window", "B) Supply/demand ratio (orders vs. available couriers)", "C) Deviation from historical baseline for same zone/day/hour"],
      "recommendation": "Option B — supply/demand ratio is the most operationally meaningful signal. Pure order volume ignores capacity. Historical deviation ignores real-time conditions. Supply/demand ratio captures the actual scarcity that should drive price."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No price-locking or quote-honoring policy exists. If a customer receives a price quote and demand changes before they confirm, it is unknown whether the quoted price is honored. This is a critical UX trust issue and potential legal requirement.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["A) Lock price for a defined TTL after quote (e.g., 5 minutes)", "B) Always charge the price at time of booking confirmation", "C) Show real-time price but honor quoted price if booking completes within a reasonable window"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — lock quoted price with a TTL. This balances customer trust with pricing accuracy. TTL duration needs product decision."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior defined for demand signal failure. If the demand data feed goes stale or unavailable, the system has no defined behavior. This is a critical operational gap for a 'fully automated' system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/SRE", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Fall back to base price", "B) Use last known demand level with a staleness decay", "C) Halt new bookings until demand signal is restored"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — fall back to base price. It is the safest default: no customer harm, no revenue loss beyond the dynamic premium, and no service interruption."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Regulatory and legal compliance is not addressed. Dynamic pricing triggers price-gouging statutes in many jurisdictions during declared emergencies. Anti-discrimination laws may apply if pricing disproportionately affects certain demographics or regions.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive Team"],
      "options": ["A) Build emergency override that caps prices during declared emergencies per jurisdiction", "B) Exempt certain jurisdictions from dynamic pricing entirely", "C) Require legal review before launch in each jurisdiction"],
      "recommendation": "Option C as prerequisite, then implement Option A. Legal review must happen before any launch. Emergency overrides should be a launch requirement, not a post-launch addition."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
